components.label: Driver App / Rider App
components.description: Driver app streams GPS pings every 4 seconds; Rider app requests ETA on ride request and receives live driver location updates

components.label: Location Ingest Service + ETA Computation Service
components.description: Location Ingest Service receives GPS pings and writes to Kafka; ETA Service reads from geo cache, runs Dijkstra on road graph, and returns estimated arrival time

components.label: Road Network Graph DB + Trip History DB
components.description: Road Network Graph stores nodes (intersections) and edges (road segments with distance/speed); Trip History DB stores completed trip data for ML model training

components.label: Geo Cache (Redis with GEO commands)
components.description: Stores live driver locations using Redis GEOADD; GEOSEARCH returns drivers within a radius in microseconds; 10-second TTL auto-expires stale driver entries

components.label: Location Ingest Queue (Kafka)
components.description: Buffers 1.25M GPS pings/sec from driver apps; Location Ingest workers consume and write to Redis geo cache; decouples ingest rate from Redis write throughput

connections.label: Ingest worker: GEOADD driver location to Redis
Load road graph segment for ETA path computation
Return road graph with real-time traffic weights

keyInsights: Geohashing is the indexing breakthrough: by encoding lat/lng into a base32 string (e.g. 'dr5ru'), all points within the same geohash cell share a common prefix — range queries become simple string prefix lookups rather than expensive 2D spatial scans

Redis GEOADD/GEOSEARCH is the production implementation: Redis stores geo points in a sorted set using the geohash as the score, enabling GEOSEARCH key FROMLONLAT lon lat BYRADIUS 2 km ASC COUNT 10 to run in O(log n + m) time

The Kafka queue between driver apps and Redis is essential: at 1.25M pings/sec, direct Redis writes would require a 1.25M ops/sec Redis cluster; Kafka absorbs bursty ingest and smooths the write rate to Redis

ETA uses a pre-computed road graph with real-time traffic overlay: the base graph (Dijkstra-ready weighted adjacency list) is pre-loaded into memory; traffic speed updates from a traffic data feed adjust edge weights every 60 seconds without reloading the full graph

TTL-based staleness is the correct design: each GEOADD sets a 10-second expiry on the driver's entry; if no ping arrives, the entry expires automatically — no explicit 'driver went offline' event is needed, eliminating a whole class of race conditions

tradeoffs.decision: Redis GEO over PostGIS for live driver locations
tradeoffs.pro: Sub-millisecond proximity queries; in-memory speed; TTL-based auto-expiry of stale drivers
tradeoffs.con: Redis is not durable by default; driver location history cannot be queried; limited to a single Redis cluster's memory capacity

tradeoffs.decision: Kafka buffer between driver apps and Redis
tradeoffs.pro: Absorbs 1.25M pings/sec bursts; decouples ingest latency from Redis write latency; enables replay for debugging
tradeoffs.con: Adds ~50-200ms latency between a driver moving and their location appearing in the geo cache; acceptable for ETA but not for turn-by-turn navigation

tradeoffs.decision: Pre-computed road graph over real-time routing API
tradeoffs.pro: ETA computed in <10ms using in-memory Dijkstra on cached graph; no external API dependency
tradeoffs.con: Graph must be refreshed periodically; does not reflect sudden road closures or accidents until next refresh cycle

antiPatterns: Querying PostgreSQL with ST_DWithin for every driver proximity search — at 10K ETA queries/sec this generates 10K complex geospatial DB queries per second; unsustainable
Storing driver locations in a relational table without geospatial indexing — full table scan for every proximity search; O(n) where n is the number of active drivers
Computing ETA by calling an external routing API (e.g. Google Maps API) per request — external API latency is 100-500ms plus cost; cannot be used at 10K queries/sec
Not implementing location staleness — without TTL-based expiry, disconnected drivers remain in the geo index indefinitely, causing ghost drivers to appear in proximity searches
